title: Carotid sinus hypersensitivity: glossopharyngeal afferent
vignette: A 65-year-old man reports multiple episodes of lightheadedness while buttoning a tight shirt collar. During 2 episodes, he passed out briefly but sustained no injuries. His blood pressure was 70/40 mm Hg and pulse was 45/min during one of the episodes. Past medical history is significant for hypertension and diet-controlled diabetes mellitus. The patient is a lifetime nonsmoker and drinks alcohol on social occasions. On physical examination, his blood pressure is 125/72 mm Hg and pulse is 76/min without orthostatic changes. Stimulation of afferent sensory fibers in which of the following nerves is most likely responsible for this patient's symptoms?

clues.text: lightheadedness while buttoning a tight shirt collar
clues.note: The trigger is mechanical pressure on the neck (a tight collar). External compression of the carotid sinus over-stimulates its baroreceptors, producing reflex bradycardia and hypotension - the defining scenario of carotid sinus hypersensitivity. The same can be provoked by neck-turning or shaving.

The transient syncope is the downstream consequence of the reflex: a sudden surge of vagal (parasympathetic) output and withdrawal of sympathetic tone drops both heart rate and blood pressure enough to cause cerebral hypoperfusion.

clues.text: blood pressure was 70/40 mm Hg and pulse was 45/min during one of the episodes
clues.note: Documented hypotension (70/40) AND bradycardia (45/min) DURING the event is the fingerprint of a vagally mediated reflex - both the cardioinhibitory (bradycardia) and vasodepressor (hypotension) limbs are firing, exactly what carotid sinus stimulation produces.

Normal vital signs with no orthostatic drop between events specifically EXCLUDES orthostatic (postural) hypotension as the cause, steering you toward a reflex triggered by carotid sinus pressure rather than volume/autonomic failure.

whatMatters: Boil it down: syncope triggered by pressure on the neck (tight collar) with bradycardia + hypotension = carotid sinus hypersensitivity. The question asks for the AFFERENT (sensory) nerve of the carotid sinus reflex - that is the glossopharyngeal nerve (CN IX, via the nerve of Hering). The efferent limb is the vagus, but they asked for the afferent - answer B.
strategy: For baroreceptor questions, split two axes: AFFERENT vs EFFERENT, and CAROTID vs AORTIC. Carotid sinus afferent = CN IX (glossopharyngeal); aortic arch afferent = CN X (vagus); the efferent limb of both reflexes is the vagus. A neck-pressure trigger (tight collar, shaving, head-turning) localizes to the carotid sinus, so the SENSORY nerve being asked for is CN IX.

eliminations.label: accessory (CN XI) - pure motor
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: the accessory nerve (CN XI) is a pure MOTOR nerve to the sternocleidomastoid and trapezius. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it carries no baroreceptor sensory fibers and has no role in this reflex. ✅ Correct if: asked what innervates head-turning (SCM) or shoulder-shrug (trapezius).

eliminations.label: hypoglossal (CN XII) - tongue motor
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: the hypoglossal nerve (CN XII) provides MOTOR innervation to the tongue muscles. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: no autonomic or baroreceptor function; a lesion causes tongue deviation, not syncope. ✅ Correct if: asked about tongue movement or deviation.

eliminations.label: trigeminal (CN V) - facial sensation/mastication
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: the trigeminal nerve (CN V) carries facial sensation and motor fibers to the muscles of mastication. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it does not carry carotid sinus afferents; wrong sensory territory entirely. ✅ Correct if: asked about facial sensation or the corneal/jaw-jerk reflex.

eliminations.label: vagus (CN X) - aortic afferent + the efferent limb (your distractor)
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: the vagus nerve (CN X) carries the AORTIC ARCH baroreceptor afferents AND is the parasympathetic EFFERENT limb of both baroreflexes. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: the trigger here is the CAROTID SINUS (neck pressure), whose AFFERENT runs in CN IX, not CN X; the vagus is the efferent in this reflex, but the question specifically asks for the afferent sensory nerve. This is the most tempting wrong answer. ✅ Correct if: the stimulus were aortic-arch stretch, or the question asked for the EFFERENT limb.

reasoning: Tight-collar trigger + syncope with documented bradycardia/hypotension = carotid sinus hypersensitivity
The reflex's sensory (afferent) limb begins at the carotid sinus baroreceptors
Carotid sinus afferents travel via the nerve of Hering into the glossopharyngeal nerve (CN IX)
mechanism: The carotid sinus (a dilation at the origin of the internal carotid artery) houses baroreceptors that sense arterial wall stretch. Their afferents run via the nerve of Hering into the GLOSSOPHARYNGEAL nerve (CN IX) to the nucleus tractus solitarius in the medulla. External pressure (tight collar, neck-turning, shaving) over-stimulates them in carotid sinus hypersensitivity, triggering increased vagal (CN X) parasympathetic output and withdrawal of sympathetic tone - producing bradycardia (cardioinhibitory) and vasodilation/hypotension (vasodepressor) and, if severe, syncope. Aortic arch baroreceptors instead send afferents via the vagus (CN X). So: carotid afferent = IX, aortic afferent = X, efferent of both = X.

difficulty.label: 📚 Pure knowledge gap
difficulty.message: First Aid Physiology/Neuro > baroreceptor reflex: carotid sinus afferent = CN IX (nerve of Hering); aortic arch afferent = CN X; efferent = vagus. Cross-ref CV syncope (carotid sinus hypersensitivity).

difficulty.label: 🧭 Understanding the question
difficulty.message: Two-part decoding: (1) which receptor (carotid sinus, from the neck-pressure trigger)? (2) afferent or efferent (the stem says 'afferent sensory fibers')? Carotid + afferent = CN IX.

difficulty.label: 🔤 Vocabulary
difficulty.message: Unsure on afferent vs efferent, baroreceptor, carotid sinus vs carotid body, nerve of Hering, cardioinhibitory vs vasodepressor, orthostatic? Tell me which and I'll explain it.

difficulty.label: 🧲 Pulled by a distractor
difficulty.message: Vagus (E) is the trap because it IS central to the reflex - but as the EFFERENT limb and as the AORTIC afferent. The carotid sinus afferent they asked about is CN IX.

difficulty.label: ✍️ Something else
difficulty.message: Lock the one-liner: 'Carotid sinus -> IX in, X out.' Tight collar squeezes the sinus, so the afferent firing is glossopharyngeal.

keywords.front: Syncope when buttoning collar / turning the neck

keywords.back: Glossopharyngeal (CN IX), via the nerve of Hering

firstAid.detail: Physio/Neuro > baroreceptors: carotid sinus (CN IX) vs aortic arch (CN X); efferent vagus; cardioinhibitory + vasodepressor responses.
firstAid.location: Physio/Neuro > baroreceptors: carotid sinus (CN IX) vs aortic arch (CN X); efferent vagus; cardioinhibitory + vasodepressor responses.

firstAid.detail: CV > syncope (reflex/neurally mediated): carotid sinus hypersensitivity triggered by neck pressure; bradycardia + hypotension.
firstAid.location: CV > syncope (reflex/neurally mediated): carotid sinus hypersensitivity triggered by neck pressure; bradycardia + hypotension.

firstAid.detail: Neuro > CN V (facial sensation/mastication), IX (carotid sinus/body, posterior tongue), X (aortic baroreceptors, parasympathetic efferent), XI (SCM/trapezius), XII (tongue) - learn each to reject the motor nerves quickly.
firstAid.location: Neuro > CN V (facial sensation/mastication), IX (carotid sinus/body, posterior tongue), X (aortic baroreceptors, parasympathetic efferent), XI (SCM/trapezius), XII (tongue) - learn each to reject the motor nerves quickly.

memoryHook: Carotid sinus: IX in (afferent), X out (efferent). A tight collar squeezes the sinus -> CN IX fires.
